steps.title: Hallo & Tot ziens
steps.desc: Your very first Dutch words, the ones you'll use every single day. Dutch greetings are logical and quick to master.
goals: Know when to use each one

steps.note:
Works with anyone, anywhere, anytime.
When in doubt: hallo.

Until about noon.
Goede + morgen = good + morning.

steps.exampleEn: Good afternoon. Can I help you?
steps.note: Noon to ~6 PM. Slightly formal — shops, offices.

From ~6 PM onwards.
Pattern: goede + time of day!

steps.title: 🧠 The Goede + Time Pattern
steps.text:
All formal greetings follow one formula:

goede + morgen = goedemorgen (good morning)
goede + middag = goedemiddag (good afternoon)
goede + avond = goedenavond (good evening)

Three words, one pattern. Done!

deepDive.title: The Dutch 'g': the hardest sound
deepDive.text:
You'll hear a distinctive throat sound in 'goedemorgen'. The Dutch 'g' is NOT like English 'g', it's a friction sound from the back of your throat.

Tips to practice:
• Start by saying 'aah' like at the doctor
• Add friction, like gentle gargling
• Practice: goed, gaan, groot, groep

In the south of the Netherlands and Belgium, the 'g' is softer, almost like a gentle 'h'. Both are correct!

steps.note:
Works as BOTH hello AND goodbye.
Often doubled for warmth: 'dag dag!'
'De dag' = the day (separate meaning).

Literally: 'until seeing.'
Formal — shops, offices, strangers.

Very casual. Like English 'bye-bye!'
Friends only.

steps.exampleEn: I have to go. See you later!
steps.note:
'Tot' = until. Dutch builds farewells with it:
tot morgen = see you tomorrow
tot zo = see you soon

steps.title: 👔 Formal vs Casual
steps.text:
🏢 Formal (shops, strangers, older people):
Goedemorgen / Goedemiddag / Tot ziens / Dag

😊 Casual (friends, family, peers):
Hallo / Hoi / Hey / Doei

When in doubt, start formal. The Dutch will quickly say 'je mag jij zeggen' (you can use informal).

deepDive.title: Why two levels of politeness?
deepDive.text:
English used to have this too! 'Thou' was informal and 'you' was formal. English dropped 'thou'. Dutch kept both.

jij/je = informal 'you' (like old English 'thou')
u = formal 'you'

The Netherlands is quite informal compared to Germany or France. Many Dutch people quickly switch to 'je'. But starting with 'u' shows respect.

Fun fact: Belgium uses 'u' MUCH more than the Netherlands!

steps.q: It's 9 AM. You enter a bakery. You say:
Leaving a business meeting, you say:

steps.title: Zijn: To Be
steps.desc: The most important verb in Dutch. You'll learn ALL forms of 'zijn' in one go, because you need them all from day one.

steps.note:
'Ik' = I (always lowercase mid-sentence).
No article before professions: 'Ik ben student.'

Use 'jij' with friends, family, peers.
In questions: flips to 'ben jij?' — the -t drops!

For strangers, elders, formal settings.
Same form as jij: bent.
'Hij' = he. Uses 'is'. Simple!

steps.also: 'zij' also means 'they': context makes it clear
steps.note: 'Zij' = she. Same form as hij: is.

steps.title: Zijn: to be
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
Only 4 forms to learn: ben, bent, is, zijn.
That covers ALL 9 pronouns!

deepDive.title: The jij/je mystery: what's the difference?
deepDive.text:
You'll see both 'jij' and 'je' everywhere. They mean the same thing!

jij = emphasized form
je = unstressed form

In daily speech, 'je' is far more common. 'Jij' adds emphasis or contrast:
• Jij bent groot = YOU are tall (not him)
• Je bent groot = you're tall (neutral)

Same pattern for all paired pronouns:
• zij / ze (she or they)
• wij / we (we)

The short forms sound more natural in everyday Dutch.

steps.note:
'Wij' = we. Uses the full form: 'zijn'.
Short form: 'we zijn'.

'Jullie' = you (plural). Also uses 'zijn'.
No separate formal plural — 'u' covers both.

Same word as 'she' — context makes it clear.
Short form: 'ze zijn'.

steps.title: 🔑 Pattern: Only 4 Forms
steps.text:
Despite 9 pronouns, zijn only has 4 forms:

ben → only ik (I)
bent → jij (you) and u (you, formal)
is → hij (he), zij (she), het (it)
zijn → wij (we), jullie (you all), zij (they)

Compare English: am, are, is, also just 3 forms!
Dutch adds one more (ben ≠ bent) but the logic is identical.

steps.hint: The -t drops when jij follows the verb
zij (they) takes the same form as wij

steps.title: Question Words
steps.desc: Six words that unlock hundreds of questions. Plus the one rule you need: Dutch flips the verb in questions.

steps.note:
Sounds like 'vee'.
Dutch W = English V sound.

steps.also: also means 'some': wat water = some water
steps.note:
W = V sound. Rhymes with 'but', not 'bat'.
Short 'a'.

steps.also: also means 'true': dat is waar! = that's true!
steps.note:
Long 'aa' — hold it!
You WILL need this word in the Netherlands.

steps.title: ⚡ Question Word Order
steps.text:
Dutch flips the verb before the subject:

Jij bent student. → Ben jij student?

With a question word, it goes FIRST:
Wie ben jij? = Who are you?
Wat is dat? = What is that?
Waar is het station? = Where is the station?

Pattern: Question word → VERB → subject → rest

deepDive.title: Why does Dutch flip the verb?
deepDive.text:
This is called 'inversion' and Dutch does it consistently.

In statements: subject → verb → rest
Jij bent groot. = You are tall.

In questions: verb → subject → rest
Ben jij groot? = Are you tall?

With question word: question → verb → subject → rest
Hoe groot ben jij? = How tall are you?

German does exactly the same thing! If you ever learn German, this rule transfers directly.

steps.exampleEn: What's your name? (lit: How are you called?)
steps.note:
THE most common way to ask a name.
Literally: 'How are-you-called?'
Two syllables: wan-NEER. Stress on second.

Waar + om = 'where about.'
Stress on second: vahr-OM.

steps.title: 🗣️ Your Survival Questions
steps.text:
Memorize these 5, they'll save you daily:

Wat is dat? = What is that?
Waar is het toilet? = Where is the toilet?
Hoe heet je? = What's your name?
Wanneer vertrekt de trein? = When does the train leave?
Waarom niet? = Why not?

Notice: verb ALWAYS comes right after the question word.

steps.q: In Dutch questions, the verb comes:

steps.title: De, Het & Een: Dutch Articles
steps.desc: Dutch has TWO words for 'the': de and het. No reliable rule — you learn them with each word.

steps.note:
'De' is the most common article (~2/3 of nouns).
All plurals ALWAYS use 'de'.

A de-word. 'v' = English 'v'.
'ouw' rhymes with 'now'.

'Het' words are ~1/3 of all nouns.
No reliable rule — learn it per word.

steps.title: 📖 De vs Het: The Honest Truth
steps.text:
There is NO reliable rule for de vs het.

✅ What helps:
• Learn each noun WITH its article (de man, het huis)
• All plurals use 'de' (de mannen, de huizen)
• Most people-words use 'de'
• Words ending in -je always use 'het'
• If you guess 'de', you're right ~65% of the time

deepDive.title: Why does Dutch have de and het?
deepDive.text:
Dutch inherited two grammatical genders from older Germanic:

de-words = originally masculine OR feminine
het-words = neuter

Over centuries, Dutch merged masculine and feminine into one group ('de') but kept neuter separate ('het'). German still has all three (der/die/das). Dutch simplified to two.

Fun fact: Dutch speakers themselves often disagree about de/het for some words! Even native speakers sometimes say 'de auto' and 'het auto' interchangeably. So don't stress too much!

steps.note:
The 'ui' is a uniquely Dutch sound — round your lips!
Doesn't exist in English.

'Een' = a/an for ALL nouns.
It NEVER changes. That's the good news!

A de-word. Most animals use 'de'.
Exception: 'het paard' (the horse).

steps.hint: De mannen, de huizen, de katten...

steps.q: Words ending in -je (like broodje, meisje) always use:
steps.hint: Het broodje, het meisje, het kopje...

steps.title: Hebben: To Have
steps.desc: Your second essential verb, all forms at once. After this lesson you know zijn AND hebben, the two verbs that power 60% of daily Dutch.

steps.note:
Spelled 'heb', sounds like 'hep'.
Final b → p, d → t, g → k in Dutch!

'Hebt' for jij.
In questions: 'Heb jij...?' — the -t drops!
Real rule, not an exception.

Some say 'u hebt' — both accepted.
'U heeft' is more standard.

Three forms: heb, hebt, heeft.
ik → no -t, jij → -t, hij → -ft.

steps.title: Hebben: to have
steps.label: Full Conjugation
steps.note:
4 forms: heb, hebt, heeft, hebben.
Same pattern as zijn!

deepDive.title: The -t drop rule: the most useful grammar trick
deepDive.text:
When jij comes AFTER the verb (in questions), the -t disappears:

Jij hebt een auto. = You have a car.
Heb jij een auto? = Do you have a car?

Jij bent groot. = You are tall.
Ben jij groot? = Are you tall?

Jij werkt hier. = You work here.
Werk jij hier? = Do you work here?

This ONLY happens with jij/je, never with u, hij, or anyone else. It's one of the first 'real' grammar rules worth mastering.

steps.also: 'honger hebben' = to be hungry (lit: to have hunger)
steps.exampleEn: We are hungry. (lit: We have hunger)
steps.note:
Dutch says 'have hunger' not 'are hungry'.
Same for thirst: 'Ik heb dorst.'
Like French and German!

Same as wij: hebben.
The plural always uses the full form.

steps.title: 🔁 Quick Review: Zijn + Hebben
steps.text:
ZIJN (to be): ben, bent, is, zijn
HEBBEN (to have): heb, hebt, heeft, hebben

• Ik ben student. = I am a student.
• Ik heb een kat. = I have a cat.
• Ben jij blij? = Are you happy?
• Hij is groot. = He is tall.

steps.hint: The -t drops when jij follows the verb

steps.title: Ja, Nee & Alsjeblieft
steps.desc: The three magic words of Dutch politeness. Master these and every interaction gets smoother, shops, cafés, trains, everywhere.

steps.note:
J in Dutch ALWAYS sounds like English Y.
'Ja, graag' = yes please — your go-to in shops.

Long 'ee' sound.
Always 'Nee, dank je' — never just 'nee' (too abrupt).

Literally 'it may seem.' Stress: mis-SCHIEN.
'sch' = 'sk' sound.

steps.title: 🙏 Alsjeblieft: The Swiss Army Word
steps.text:
'Alsjeblieft' is used THREE ways:

1. As 'please':
Mag ik een koffie, alsjeblieft? = Can I have a coffee, please?

2. As 'here you go' (handing something):
Alsjeblieft! = Here you go!

3. As 'you're welcome':
Dank je! → Alsjeblieft! = Thanks! → You're welcome!

One word, three functions. Context makes it clear.

deepDive.title: How 'alsjeblieft' is a compressed sentence
deepDive.text:
It looks like one word but it's actually a full sentence!

als + je + belieft = 'if it pleases you'

• als = if
• je = you (informal)
• belieft = pleases (archaic Dutch verb)

The formal version 'alstublieft' works the same way:
als + t + u + belieft = 'if it pleases you' (formal)

The 't' comes from the formal verb conjugation. Most Dutch people don't realize this, you'll impress them if you know it!

steps.note:
Literally 'if it pleases you' (als + je + belieft).
Also means 'here you go' when handing something over.

Formal version: als + u + belieft.
The je/u swap is the only difference.

Add 'wel' for emphasis: 'dank je wel!'
You'll hear 'dankjewel' as one word.

je → u is the formality switch.
'Dank u wel' for emphasis.
Also common: 'bedankt' (works for both).

steps.title: 🔑 The je/u Pattern
steps.text:
• alsjeblieft / alstublieft = please
• dank je / dank u = thank you
• hoe heet je / hoe heet u = what's your name
• hoe gaat het met je / met u = how are you

'je' ↔ 'u' is the ONLY switch. Works for ANY phrase.

steps.note:
Borrowed from English — same meaning.
'Pardon' also works (slightly more formal).

Incredibly versatile:
ja, graag = yes please
graag gedaan = you're welcome
ik wil graag... = I would like...

steps.q: A waiter hands you coffee. You say:
steps.hint: They gave you something: you thank them

steps.q: You hand a friend their book back. You say:
The formal version of 'dank je' is:

steps.title: Numbers 1–10
steps.desc: Dutch numbers 1–10 are your everyday toolkit, prices, times, addresses, phone numbers. Most are recognizable if you speak English or German.

steps.note:
As number: stressed 'EEN koffie.'
As article (a/an): unstressed 'een koffie.'
In writing: 'één' (with accents) = the number.
steps.deepDive:
Dutch uses accent marks to tell them apart:

één = the number one (één koffie = one coffee)
een = the article a/an (een koffie = a coffee)

In speech, stress does the job.
In writing, the accents do.
You'll see 'één' on menus, signs, and clocks.

steps.note:
'tw' sounds like English 'tv'.
Lips together, then release.

Like English 'three' without the 'th'.
Dutch 'r' is slightly rolled.

Sounds like 'fear.' Dutch v = English f:
vier/four, vader/father, vis/fish.

'ij' = uniquely Dutch, like 'ay' in 'say'.
Rhymes with English 'knife'.

steps.title: 🧠 Germanic Cousins
steps.text:
Dutch numbers are siblings of English and German:

• een = one (German: eins)
• twee = two (German: zwei)
• drie = three (German: drei)
• vier = four (German: vier)
• vijf = five (German: fünf)

You already know these, your brain just needs to connect the dots!

steps.note: Almost identical to English 'says'. Easy!

Dutch 'v' = English 'v'.
Compare: seven / zeven / sieben.
'ch' = same throat sound as Dutch 'g'.
The 'g' has the Dutch throat sound.
Sounds like English 'teen'.

steps.title: 💰 Numbers in Real Life
steps.text:
You'll use these instantly:

Dat is drie euro vijftig. = That's €3.50.
Twee koffie, alsjeblieft. = Two coffees, please.
Het is vijf uur. = It's five o'clock.
Nummer acht. = Number eight.

Dutch people use numbers constantly!

steps.q: How do you say 'five' in Dutch?
'Een' as a number is pronounced differently than 'een' as:

steps.title: Introducing Yourself
steps.desc: Put it all together, you'll learn to introduce yourself in Dutch with a natural mini-conversation. This is where L1–L7 click into place.

steps.en: my name is... (lit: I am called)
steps.note:
'Heten' = to be called.
More natural than 'mijn naam is'.

Literally: 'How are-you-called?'
'hoe' = how, not what. Verb flips in questions.

steps.exampleEn: Nice to meet you! I'm Mark.
steps.note:
Slightly formal but universally appropriate.
Literally: 'pleasant'.

The glue word of Dutch.
'En jij?' = and you? — bounces any question back.

steps.example: Ik spreek Engels, maar ik leer Nederlands.
steps.exampleEn: I speak English, but I'm learning Dutch.
steps.note:
The second connector you need.
'En' links, 'maar' contrasts.
'Ik ben klein maar sterk.' = I am small but strong.

steps.title: 🗣️ Your First Dutch Conversation
steps.text:
A: Hallo! Hoe heet je? = Hi! What's your name?
B: Ik heet Sophie. En jij? = I'm Sophie. And you?
A: Ik heet Jan. Aangenaam! = I'm Jan. Nice to meet you!
B: Aangenaam! = Nice to meet you!

That's it, you just introduced yourself in Dutch!

deepDive.title: 'En jij?': the power of bouncing back
deepDive.text:
In any language, the fastest way to keep a conversation going is to bounce the question back:

Hoe heet je? = What's your name?
Ik heet... En jij? = I'm called... And you?

Hoe gaat het? = How are you?
Goed! En met jou? = Good! And with you?

Waar woon je? = Where do you live?
Ik woon in... En jij? = I live in... And you?

'En jij?' (and you?) and 'en met jou?' (and with you?) are your conversation lifelines.

Note: 'jou' is the object form of 'jij', like English 'me' vs 'I'. More on this in Unit 2!

steps.note:
'Uit' = from/out of.
The 'ui' is that Dutch diphthong again!

'Vandaan' = from-there.
'Waar kom je uit?' also works, but 'vandaan' is more natural.

'Wonen' = to live (reside).
W = V sound. 'Woon' rhymes with 'bone'.

steps.example: Sorry, ik spreek een beetje Nederlands.
steps.exampleEn: Sorry, I speak a little Dutch.
steps.note:
THE survival sentence. Dutch people love hearing this!
'Een beetje' = a little bit.

steps.title: 🎬 The Full Introduction
steps.text: You know every word you need for a natural Dutch introduction: name, origin, residence. Tap below to see a full model conversation.

deepDive.title: Model conversation
deepDive.text:
A: Hallo! Ik heet Jan. Hoe heet jij?
→ Hi! I'm Jan. What's your name?

B: Ik heet Sophie. Aangenaam!
→ I'm Sophie. Nice to meet you!

A: Waar kom je vandaan?
→ Where are you from?

B: Ik kom uit België. En jij?
→ I'm from Belgium. And you?

A: Ik kom uit Nederland. Ik woon in Utrecht.
→ I'm from the Netherlands. I live in Utrecht.

B: Leuk! Ik woon in Antwerpen.
→ Nice! I live in Antwerp.

You know every word here. You are ready.

steps.q: Someone asks 'Hoe heet je?', you reply:
'Waar kom je vandaan?' asks about your:

steps.hint: What do you do with a language?

steps.q: Dutch people react to 'ik spreek een beetje Nederlands' by:
steps.hint: They love it when foreigners try!

steps.title: Niet & Geen
steps.desc: Dutch has TWO words for negation: 'niet' (not) and 'geen' (no/not a). One simple rule covers 90% of cases.

steps.note:
Negates verbs and adjectives.
Goes AFTER the verb: 'Ik woon niet in Amsterdam.'

steps.exampleEn: I don't have a car. (lit: I have no car)
steps.note:
Replaces 'een' (a/an) to negate nouns.
'Ik heb een auto' → 'Ik heb geen auto.'

steps.title: ⚡ The Golden Rule: Niet vs Geen
steps.text:
Would you use 'een' (a/an) in the positive sentence?

✅ YES → use GEEN:
• Ik heb een hond → Ik heb GEEN hond.
• Dat is een probleem → Dat is GEEN probleem.

❌ NO → use NIET:
• Ik ben groot → Ik ben NIET groot.
• Hij woont in Utrecht → Hij woont NIET in Utrecht.

One rule, 90% of cases.

deepDive.title: Where does 'niet' go in the sentence?
deepDive.text:
Placement of 'niet' follows these patterns:

After the verb:
Ik zwem niet. = I don't swim.

Before adjectives:
Het is niet groot. = It's not big.

Before prepositional phrases:
Ik woon niet in Amsterdam. = I don't live in Amsterdam.

At the end if nothing else:
Ik weet het niet. = I don't know.

Common beginner mistake:
❌ Ik niet woon in Amsterdam.
✅ Ik woon niet in Amsterdam.

The verb stays in position 2. 'Niet' comes after it.

steps.note:
Niet AFTER 'ben', BEFORE adjective.
'Ik ben niet groot, niet klein...'

steps.exampleEn: I don't have time. (lit: I have no time)
steps.note:
'Geen' goes where 'een' would go.
geen tijd, geen geld, geen probleem!

One of the most useful Dutch phrases.
You'll say this ten times a day.

steps.title: 🔁 Niet & Geen with Zijn + Hebben
steps.text:
ZIJN + niet:
Ik ben niet Nederlands. = I'm not Dutch.
Hij is niet hier. = He is not here.

HEBBEN + geen:
Ik heb geen geld. = I have no money.
Wij hebben geen kinderen. = We don't have children.

Pattern: zijn pairs with 'niet', hebben pairs with 'geen'.

steps.q: 'Ik heb ___ auto.' (I don't have a car.)
steps.hint: Would the positive use 'een'? Ik heb een auto → ...
Verb stays in position 2, niet comes after

steps.title: Unit 1: Everything Together
steps.desc: Time to prove you own this. No new words, just L1–L9 mixed together. If you nail this, you're ready for Unit 2.

steps.title: 📋 What You Know Now
steps.text:
After 9 lessons you can:

• Greet anyone (formal + casual)
• Say goodbye four different ways
• Say all forms of zijn and hebben
• Ask who, what, where, how, when, why
• Use de, het, and een correctly
• Be polite (alsjeblieft, dank je)
• Count to ten
• Introduce yourself naturally
• Say 'no' with niet and geen

That's a LOT for Unit 1. Let's test it.

steps.q: It's 3 PM. You enter a shop. You say:
'Wij ___ blij.', Fill in the verb (to be):

steps.hint: One of the het-words from L4

steps.title: Zijn: to be
steps.label: Review
steps.note: 4 forms: ben, bent, is, zijn.

steps.title: Hebben: to have
steps.label: Review
steps.note: 4 forms: heb, hebt, heeft, hebben.

steps.q: You hand someone their change. You say:

steps.hint: Negating a noun: would use 'een' in positive?

steps.hint: Student has an implied 'een': so use geen
